sentences.text: We tried everything possible.
So one day, my husband said, I think I can get a hold of a German uniform.
If I can try to get with the train, I'll see what happens in Poland.
When it was empty they came, and then the trains were filled.
The wagons were filled, and those Germans went back with them to Poland.
So he tried to ask questions to find out what-- what was really going on there.
I think was the [?
Jew ?] first.
He tried it too.
And it made me so nervous.
Oh, I had a glass of red wine, and I threw it all over the table.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But you never found out.
We were told those people never entered the camp.
They went with the train, and then they had to go away.
And the empty train they accompanied again to Westerbork.
But one said, [GERMAN], and that told us very bad.
But he never said what it was.
[GERMAN].
But we tried everything.
And after the war, where we heard England knew about it.
America knew about it.
I've always said, well, then it's on their heads, too, what happened to the Jews.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They should have made it world-- world news.
We tried.
We tried to have the line blown up.
But Westerbork wasn't all that far from Germany, and they would have forced the people to walk to Germany and the trains would have been there.
We could have [INAUDIBLE] that train.
Much ?] probably.
So we talked about it and we said, well, what's the use?
What can we win with it?
No, no, we never did.
What we did was what I say, helping the people in the camp, try to let them-- in the beginning, it was easy to let them leave the camp.
It was a bit chaotic, so they could walk out.
Then we had little passes that we could get them out of the camp.
Now, for instance, we had a group in the camp, the [NON-ENGLISH].
They had a band around the arm with a F and a C on it.
Well, we could get those bands.
So I handed them out to young boys, and outside the camp they threw them away.
[NON-ENGLISH] was the group who looked after [INAUDIBLE] that they were brought to the people when the parcels came in.
They had dropped parcels.
They were handed out by the [NON-ENGLISH].
So at that time, you could give him-- them those bands, and they could walk out of it.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then my husband went to the [NON-ENGLISH] to [? pull ?] the cards out, threw-- tore them up.
Then they weren't in the camp anymore.
So they [? were ?] registered at that moment, and they-- they could stay in the camp.
They were hiding in the camp because they were not known.
They couldn't be sent on transport, and that was what we wanted.
But later on, it got much more dangerous.
If-- when then someone fled, the relatives were taken, or people in their barracks were taken.
So that was dangerous to those people.
But in the beginning, it wasn't all that difficult.
Always the Jews.
Get into the trains.
That was-- that were all German Jews-- [NON-ENGLISH].
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The Germans had it organized all right.
They let the Jews make the lists.
They only tell them, we want thousands, or we want so many.
And the Jews had to pick the people themselves, had to bring them to the trains.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And later on, Jews said, we didn't want to do it.
But we were told, if you don't do it, you're in the next train.
So we did it to save our own lives.
That's difficult.
In the beginning, it was easy.
So there were a lot of people you could help to walk out of the camp.
Later on, we looked for hiding places ourselves, and we found them mainly in [NON-ENGLISH].
And Mr.
[NON-ENGLISH], [NON-ENGLISH] came every week in the camp, and his wife brought him away on a tandem-- double bicycle.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So we only had to take care that they came outside the camp, and she brought him to a hiding place.
Out camp-- outside the camp.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] later on, it was very, very hard to find hiding places.
People were scared too.
When they were found out, they were sent straight away to a concentration camp.
So it wasn't easy for those people either.
Myself?
No, I couldn't help any, but my husband did--
--more, perhaps [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] 30, 40.
I'm not sure.
The will to help, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] to work against the Germans as much as you could.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] Yeah, if we didn't do it, no one did it.
So we saw it as our task that we are-- we are placed there, and I still think God saved us to help.
And I think it must have been we were already living outside, so it must have been after July '44, and it became so dangerous.
So I thought I have to do something about the girls.
The greengrocer who came in, he was [INAUDIBLE].
And I said to him, [NON-ENGLISH], promise me, when they pick us two up, take care that the-- the kids come outside the camp.
And he said, I'll build them in between the crates.
So as soon as I hear that you have been taken away, I'll come over and I'll take the girls out.
So that was my biggest or my anxious moments, that they would do something with the girls.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
But we stayed there, so.
We have often said, why didn't-- didn't he send you to a concentration camp?
But [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] they had their [NON-ENGLISH] in time.
They had everything they wanted to.
They knew when they sent my husband out who would replace him.
So it was in there, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] it was the best way for them when you stayed in-- in the camp.
And when the-- on the 11th of April, '45, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were-- we-- we knew already beforehand that they were losing.
So the end must be near.
And my husband had been to Westerbork, township of Westerbork, 8 kilometers from us.
And he had made contact with the Canadians and told them what to do when the end was there.
And so on the 11th of April I was sitting outside with my parents, beautiful weather.
And came a little girl to me, a [NON-ENGLISH], little girl was helping.
She said, Mrs. van As, don't be afraid.
Everything will be all right.
And I knew that my husband already had talked for days with people to save the-- the tower where the-- they cared for the [? warming ?] installation for the hot water systems.
And they-- they were afraid they would explode those towers so they couldn't [? work ?] the installations anymore.
And my husband had put a [NON-ENGLISH] on it, and it said, take care that they don't get the chance to have that blown up.
When this little girl came, I said, uh-uh, then they still get the chance to blow it up.
So what did I do?
I took all the pictures from the wall where-- with glass, put it straight on the floor.
And glassware-- glasses from the cupboard, in a [NON-ENGLISH], I put them all there.
And my mother said, don't you think it will be time to go inside to make some dinner?
I said, ah, beautiful weather.
We'll stay outside a bit longer.
But then it was close to 6 o'clock.
And I thought, I want to know what's going on.
So I walked away up to the office.
And then I had to go past the Germans.
And they were still sitting in front of the garden, drinking and-- and I thought, I hope they're not shooting me in my back, because we knew too much.
So I walked past.
And when I came in the office, my husband said, what are you doing here?
Now you can't get-- can't get out.
I said, oh, come off it.
I'll come out, don't you worry.
He said, no, I can't get out either.
I said, but I come out.
So when I went back, there was a post, and an old, old German, old chappy, he said, lady.
I said, go outside.
I have to go.
And he let me go.
So I walk again [NON-ENGLISH] alongside the-- the Germans.
And I never said, hello, [NON-ENGLISH].
I always ignored him.
And when I came home I thought, I'll go upstairs.
I want to see when they are leaving.
And I think about half past 6:00, there they went by car, very, very slowly.
I think they couldn't leave their good life behind.
They had a good life, those Germans.
So-- and nothing had exploded.
So I said to my mother, now I can eat.
So we made-- made something to eat.
And then my husband came, and he said, quick, get out of the house.
Because our house was the last going to it's-- the [? farm, ?] but going to Westerbork too.
And he knew the Canadians would come, and there might be fighting.
And our house was then the first one they could hide behind.
So he said, you better come inside.
So we went inside the camp, and my mother cried.
And I said, don't you try to cry.
The Jews have been here for years.
And that you have been here for one night doesn't mean you have a right to cry.
Don't cry.
So we were brought into the hospital, and we slept there that night.
And during the night, an woman came to me and she said, Mrs. van As, don't be afraid.
If we have to flee, we look after the kids.
So I said, I'm not afraid.
Go back to sleep.
Now, on the next day, we were freed.
The Canadians came late.
We were waiting and waiting and waiting, and you saw those planes go overhead.
And I think it was about 3 o'clock, then the Canadians came.
When the tanks came in, I didn't see a Canadian [? at all. ?] As soon as the Jews heard that they were Canadians, my husband-- they were a bit unruly.
The Germans were gone, and the Jews felt themselves free at that moment.
So they start to do silly things-- [? running ?] the cars.
And so my husband thought, that's not good what's happening now.
I'll call them all together.
But I didn't know.
So he called them all in the what we called the [NON-ENGLISH] and lights, a room and lights building, and he was talking to them there.
But the children got hungry.
And I thought, well, I better have a look to get them something to eat, because all the people were going-- going around me.
So I went to-- to the camp and kitchen was locked.
House of the main chef was locked.
And I walked a bit further, and there I saw a girl who [INAUDIBLE] worked for my husband, Hannah [NON-ENGLISH].
And I said, Hannah, what's going on here?
And she said, don't you know?
Your husband called all the people together, and they're in the big hall.
And I was heavy with Adrian.
And while I was standing there, the phone rang.
And she picked up the phone and she said, ooh, the [?
Tommies ?] are there.
So she ran away to the big hall.
And before I could say boo, the Jews came jumping out of windows, out of doors, and I was saving myself.
And they ran out of the camp onto the road towards the tanks.
And when they came inside, they were all covered in Jews.
So I said, where are those Canadians?
Yeah, some were in between.
[LAUGHS] And then they called my husband out to hoist the flag, and they carried him around on their shoulders, and they kissed me from all sides.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yeah, it was good time.
That must have-- that must have been-- that must have been a wonderful day.
That was wonderful, wonderful.
Oh, they're small.
Well, my husband promised to bring the Jews back as-- to their own places of where they had lived, and he did that.
And they ask him for the Nazis [INAUDIBLE].
You could send the Nazis to [INAUDIBLE] an [INAUDIBLE] island.
But I don't want anything to do with the Nazis.
So he help bringing Jews back.
Pardon?
Well, our war was ended at the 12th of April, but the rest of Holland was freed by the 5th of May.
So the Allied troops asked not to let the Jews go home, because they still needed the roads to pull up to Germany.

So the Jews were asked to stay in the camp, and try to work at it was were before, that everyone got their food and their laundry and-- And thereafter, the peoples who were living close by had a chance to go back.
But the ones, for instance, from Amsterdam, they didn't have any food whatsoever in Amsterdam themselves.
So there even came planes to drop food in Amsterdam and Rotterdam.
So after that, my husband tried to get trucks to bring the people back, and that wasn't easy because there were hardly any trucks in Holland left.
In the beginning of the war, the Germans stole everything what they could steal.
And then you got that there weren't tires anymore, and there wasn't petrol anymore.
And so that wasn't easy either.
But when the last left, he said, so I've done my task here, and now I do something else.
So he went to another department, that's department of [INAUDIBLE].
Oh, yes.
Mm-hmm.
We kept a lot of the Jewish friends.
Yeah, we still do here.
For instance, [NON-ENGLISH], he lives in [NON-ENGLISH].
He came there in October '42.
And when he left the camp, he went back to The Hague where he lived, and we were going to The Hague, where my husband got another job.
And in The Hague is where all the departments are, and my husband got a job with another department.
So we kept seeing the [NON-ENGLISH].
Then he went to Australia, and we saw them here again.
I think it must have been beginning of '55.
First we were going to America.
I had two brothers living there.
Was very, very hard to get into America.
And then the chap from the Immigration Office came over to our place and he said, listen, we have tried so hard to get you to America.
Why don't you go to Australia?
And we said, Australia?
We don't know Australia at all.
And he said, well, they really want people there and a good climate.
I would advise you to go to Australia.
My husband was never happy in Holland.
He was born in Indonesia.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] If our plans had been fulfilled, we would have gone in August '40, 1940, to Indonesia.
So he said, well, perhaps it's right to go to Australia.
It's more a climate like Indonesia than Holland is.
So we put our names down for Australia, I think it was July '45, and the end of October, we sailed.
One thing I meant to ask you, when did you find out what had happened to the people on the transports?
Straight after the war.
The first one who came back was [NON-ENGLISH], a lawyer herself, daughter of Professor [NON-ENGLISH].
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she came back, and she told what happened there.
We were sick for days.
We-- we couldn't understand.
I still can't understand how anybody can think out a plan to gas people.
You must be crazy.
I don't know exactly.
We have been there five times.
Don't ask me the dates.
I-- I don't know it.
Yeah.
Despair.
Same what we felt with the trains.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] still, I-- I can't-- can't imagine how anybody can do what happened in Germany.
And they don't have to tell me they didn't know.
They must have known a lot more than we did.
And if-- if they had made world known, America or England, what happened to the Jews in Poland, those Jews wouldn't have gone into the trains.
They would have fought themselves to death.
But they wouldn't have gone meekly into a train and let themselves drive for three days to be gassed.
No one would have gone.
But they didn't know.
They were tricked.
It was the idea we have to work there.
One day, a chap was living in the room next to where we were sitting behind the barbed wire.
And he said, next time I am on transport, nobody should try to get me out.
I'm going to Poland.
I don't be afraid.
He said, I can work, healthy climate there.
It doesn't matter.
I'm going.
He never came back.
No.
Well, that's it, because they didn't know.
More, ?] I have learned the in the camp, because when the train left they nearly always sang the
Yeah.
And they were waving, yeah, the little window in-- high up in the cattle wagon.
And when the door wasn't closed yet, they were waving in front of the door.
But I've learned the there.
